paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1128 English Language — Boon Lay Secondary School Preliminary 2021 SA2 Paper 2 §B

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about Nori's arrival at her grandparents' house in Kyoto, Japan, and answer the questions that follow. Section B [20 marks] from Boon Lay Secondary School Preliminary Examination 2021 Paper 1128/02. The original Section B carries 11 questions worth 20 marks (Q5-Q15); this fixture ingests the 7 short-answer items that have a single objective canonical answer plus 4 MCQ items derived from Q15's stages-of-arrival flowchart task. The 3-mark language analysis Q5, the simile-table Q7 and Q11, the literary-device Q10, and one half of the two-part Q8 are omitted as they require open-ended marker judgement.
sections.passageTitle: Nori's arrival at her grandparents' house in Kyoto (Fifty Words for Rain by Asha Lemmie)
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
Nori would say that her life had officially begun the day she laid eyes on the imposing property that rested serenely between the crests of two green hills. Stunningly beautiful, the house exuded magnificence at the height of its glory, and yet, despite this beauty, Nori felt her stomach clench and her gut churn. Her mother rarely took her anywhere, and somehow seeing the building – a blend of mystery and formidability – she knew that something was waiting for her there that she would not like.

Paragraph 2
The faded blue automobile skidded to a stop on the street across from the estate. There were words engraved at the top of the main gate, embossed in gold lettering. 'Okaasan, what do those letters say?' The woman seated beside her let out a stifled sigh. It was clear that she'd been a great beauty in her day. She was still gorgeous, but her young face was beginning to reflect the toll life had taken on her. She would not meet her daughter's gaze. 'Kamiza,' she answered at last. 'It says Kamiza.' 'That's our name, isn't it?' Nori chirped, her curiosity immediately piqued. Her mother let out a strangled giggle that made the hair on the back of Nori's neck stand up. 'Yes,' she responded softly, eyes alight with a strange look that Nori's limited vocabulary did not have the means to name. 'That is our family name. This is where my mother and father live, child. Your grandparents.'

Paragraph 3
Nori felt her heartbeat quicken. Her mother had never before made any mention of relatives or family. Indeed, the two of them had drifted along in solitude so long it seemed strange to Nori that they could actually be anchored to a tangible place. 'Did you live here once, Okaasan?' 'Once,' her mother said dryly. 'Before you were born. A long time ago.' Nori scrunched her face up in a frown. 'Why did you leave?' 'That's enough questions now, Noriko. Get your things. Come.' Nori obeyed, biting her lip to refrain from inquiring further. Her mother did not like questions. For the first time since she'd been roused at dawn that morning, Nori noticed that her mother was not carrying any bags.

Paragraph 4
'Noriko,' she said, with such unusual affection saturating her tone that it left Nori in near disbelief, 'I want you to make me a promise. Promise me you will obey.' The request caught her off guard. Not because it was unlike something her mother would say, but because not once in her life had Nori ever disobeyed. It didn't seem like something that needed to be requested. Her confusion must have been evident as her mother turned and knelt down so that they were nearly eye level.

Paragraph 5
'Noriko,' she said, with an urgency Nori had never heard before. 'Promise me. Promise me that you will obey in all things. Do no question. Do not resist. Do not think if thinking will lead you somewhere you ought not to be. Only smile and do as you are told. Only your life is more important than your obedience. Promise me this.' Nori thought to herself that this conversation was very odd. A thousand questions burned her tongue. She swallowed them back and nodded.

Paragraph 6
'Now listen. You will go inside the gate, Nori. Your grandparents will ask you your name. They will smile and you will tell them that I did not tell you. That you don't know. Do you understand?' Nori felt her mouth begin to go dry. Her heart fluttered against her chest, like a little bird trying to escape a cage. 'Okaasan, where are you going? Aren't you coming with me?' Nori's voice began to scale up in panic. 'Nori, hush. You have no reason to cry. Stop crying this instant!' She felt the tears that had begun to well recede inside her eye sockets with frightening speed. It seemed that they too were bound to obey. Her mother hesitated, searching for words for several long moments. Finally, she decided there were none and settled for patting her daughter twice on the top of the head. 'I'll watch you go. Go on. Get your things.'

Paragraph 7
Noriko picked up her belongings and proceeded slowly towards the gate which towered menacingly over her. Her steps grew smaller and smaller as she inched disbelievingly up the walkway. She turned in desperation to her mother, who by now had made her way back to the car.

Paragraph 8
'Okaasan...' Nori whimpered, as noiseless as she could. She wanted to run back to her mother, but something kept her pinned to the spot. That something held her there, relentless and pitiless in the strength of its grasp. It did not let her move, nor breathe, nor cry out, nor as she watched her mother give her one last, strangely bright gaze before getting back into the car and shutting the door behind her. She could not so much as blink as she watched the car speed down the street, around the corner, and out of sight.

(Adapted from Fifty Words for Rain by Asha Lemmie)

questions.stem: Q6. Which one word in Paragraph 2 suggests Nori's mother encountered hardship after leaving her family home? [1]
Q8. In Paragraph 4, give one thing Nori's mother did that was out of character. Focus on her tone when speaking to Nori. [1]
questions.answer: she spoke to Nori with unusual affection

questions.stem: Q9. 'It didn't seem like something that needed to be requested.' (lines 33-34). What does this sentence suggest about how Nori had been brought up since young? [1]
questions.answer: Nori's mother was very strict with her, so obedience was expected and did not need to be requested

questions.stem: Q12. In Paragraph 6, give an example of how Nori had been well-trained to follow all instructions. [1]
questions.answer: although she wanted to cry, her tears receded inside her eye sockets at her mother's command to stop crying

questions.stem: Q13. In Paragraph 8, what made Nori appear pitiful as she watched her mother leave? [1]
questions.answer: she was whimpering yet she could not run back to her mother or cry out for her

questions.stem: Q14(a). 'That something held her there, relentless and pitiless in the strength of its grasp. It did not let her move, nor breathe, nor cry out…' (lines 59-61). What do you think 'that' and 'it' could be? [1]
questions.answer: her obedience or the promise to obey her mother

questions.stem: Q14(b). What does the answer to Q14(a) suggest about Nori's character? [1]
questions.answer: she had strong self-control and willpower

sections.instruction: Q15. The structure of the text reflects the different stages of Nori's arrival at her grandparents' house. Complete the flow chart by choosing one phrase from the box below to summarise the stage in each part of the text. Phrase bank: a terrifying realisation / piling on the pressure / appealing for understanding / impending sense of doom / eagerness to find out / a display of strength / a quiet acceptance. The original task (Q15, [4]) has four blanks for Paragraph 1, Paragraphs 2-3, Paragraphs 4-5, and Paragraphs 6-8. Each blank is split into its own MCQ here so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same Fifty Words for Rain narrative in Exercise 1 above. The mood across the text shifts as the day unfolds — from initial unease at the imposing house, to childish curiosity about the family name, to the mother's escalating insistence on a promise of obedience, ending with Nori realising she has been left behind. Pick the single best phrase from the bank for each section.

questions.stem: Q15(i). What stage of Nori's arrival is best described in Paragraph 1 (Nori first laying eyes on the imposing property and sensing her stomach clench)? [1]
Q15(ii). What stage of Nori's arrival is best described in Paragraphs 2-3 (Nori curiously asking about Kamiza, the family name, and whether her mother once lived there)? [1]
Q15(iii). What stage of Nori's arrival is best described in Paragraphs 4-5 (the mother repeatedly demanding 'Promise me' that Nori will obey in all things)? [1]
Q15(iv). What stage of Nori's arrival is best described in Paragraphs 6-8 (Nori realising her mother is leaving her behind, watching the car speed away)? [1]
